register_notice: Internal only — never linked from the public dashboard. See dfapti/rules/factualism-audit.md, 'Target Visibility Gate'. A candidate is promoted to a public case only once at least one source_evidence entry is independently verified to the same bar as an ACCEPTED evidence record.

candidates.name: Canva Pty Ltd

candidates.name: JustAnswer LLC

source_evidence.title: JustAnswer in Court for alleged misleading prices, subscription trap and government affiliation claims

candidates.public_interest_reason: Subject of Federal Court proceedings brought by the ACCC (ACCC v JustAnswer LLC, VID1259 of 2025), ordered to pay $10 million in penalties for allegedly misleading consumers about subscription pricing (a '$2' offer that led to undisclosed ongoing charges of $50-90/month) and falsely implying affiliation with the Fair Work Ombudsman.
risk_indicators: Ordered by the Federal Court to pay $10 million in penalties in ACCC v JustAnswer LLC (VID1259 of 2025)
ACCC alleged misleading pricing representations and false government-affiliation claims

candidates.name: Vacaciones eDreams, S.L

source_evidence.title: Online travel agency eDreams pays penalties and gives undertaking over allegedly misleading subscription prices

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid $59,400 in ACCC infringement notices and gave a court-enforceable s.87B undertaking over allegedly misleading subscription pricing practices (advertising a subscription as '$0.00'/'FREE' without adequately disclosing conditions, and not prominently displaying total annual cost).
risk_indicators: Paid $59,400 in ACCC infringement notices for allegedly misleading subscription pricing claims
Gave the ACCC a court-enforceable undertaking (s.87B) admitting conduct contravened or likely contravened the Australian Consumer Law

risk_indicators: Paid $39,600 in ACCC infringement notices for allegedly misleading strikethrough pricing claims

candidates.name: Energex

source_evidence.title: Administrative undertakings and penalties paid by Energex and TasNetworks for alleged breaches of life support obligations

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid a penalty (reported as $60,000 in one AER source; an earlier related release cites $100,000/$140,000 total across the two distributors — treat the exact split as MEDIUM pending direct document confirmation) for alleged breaches of life support customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Rules — obligations that exist specifically to protect customers dependent on electricity supply for life support equipment.
risk_indicators: Alleged breach of life support customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Rules, resulting in an AER penalty and administrative undertaking

candidates.name: TasNetworks

source_evidence.title: Administrative undertakings and penalties paid by Energex and TasNetworks for alleged breaches of life support obligations

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid a penalty (reported as $80,000 in one AER source; figures vary slightly across related AER releases — treat as MEDIUM pending direct document confirmation) for alleged breaches of life support customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Rules.
risk_indicators: Alleged breach of life support customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Rules, resulting in an AER penalty and administrative undertaking

candidates.name: AGL Retail Energy Limited

source_evidence.title: AGL penalised $25 million for breaches of overcharging rules related to Centrepay payments

candidates.public_interest_reason: One of four AGL Energy Limited subsidiaries the Federal Court found breached overcharging rules relating to Centrepay payments (a government bill-paying service for Centrelink recipients), resulting in the largest total penalty ($25 million) ever imposed for breaches of the National Energy Laws. 483 Centrepay customers were overcharged and 16,156 breaches found for failing to inform/refund within required timeframes. The other three subsidiary entities named in the same action were not independently identified by name in the sources reviewed this pass, so are not separately recorded as candidates.
risk_indicators: Found by the Federal Court to have breached National Energy Retail Rules overcharging obligations relating to Centrepay (welfare-payment) customers
Subject to the largest total penalty ($25 million) ever imposed for National Energy Laws breaches
Decision under appeal to the Full Federal Court as of the source reviewed (heard November 2025, pending)

candidates.name: Alinta Energy Retail Sales Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Alinta fined $1.089 million for alleged mishandling of Centrepay payments

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid $1,089,000 in AER penalties (a record 15 infringement notices) for allegedly overcharging Centrepay customers (a government bill-paying service for Centrelink recipients) across NSW, Qld and SA between 2019-2024, and failing to inform/refund them within required timeframes.
risk_indicators: Paid $1,089,000 in AER infringement notice penalties for alleged Centrepay overcharging and failure to timely inform/refund welfare-payment customers

candidates.name: CAM Engineering and Construction Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: CAM Engineering and Construction Pty Ltd ordered to pay penalty for failing to join Energy and Water Ombudsman NSW scheme

candidates.public_interest_reason: Ordered by the Federal Court to pay a $250,000 penalty for allegedly failing to become a member of the Energy and Water Ombudsman NSW scheme, in breach of the National Energy Retail Law — a consumer-access-to-redress compliance matter.
risk_indicators: Ordered by the Federal Court to pay a $250,000 penalty for alleged failure to join a mandatory ombudsman scheme under the National Energy Retail Law

candidates.name: OnePath Custodians Pty Limited

source_evidence.title: APRA issues $10.7 million infringement notices and accepts court enforceable undertaking from OPC

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid $10,704,600 under APRA infringement notices for alleged breaches of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 (failing to invest members' default superannuation contributions in MySuper products), and gave APRA a court enforceable undertaking to rectify compliance deficiencies and compensate members. OPC is one of four superannuation trustees owned by Insignia Financial Ltd (formerly IOOF Holdings Limited), with approximately 700,000 members and over $37 billion in funds under management — a matter with direct financial consequences for a large number of superannuation members.
risk_indicators: Paid $10,704,600 under APRA infringement notices for alleged SIS Act breaches (failure to direct default contributions to MySuper products)
Gave APRA a court enforceable undertaking to rectify compliance deficiencies and compensate affected members

candidates.name: Union Standard International Group Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 26-117MR Federal Court orders record $300 million penalties in ASIC's case over 'egregious' Union Standard and CFD operator misconduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: Collapsed CFD issuer found by the Federal Court to have engaged in systemic unconscionable conduct (with its former authorised representatives) between 2018-2020; ASIC's media release describes penalties totalling $300.2 million as the highest ever secured in an ASIC matter, with customer losses exceeding $83 million.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court-ordered penalties totalling $300.2 million (ASIC's largest-ever recorded penalty in a single matter) for systemic unconscionable conduct 2018-2020

source_evidence.title: 26-117MR Federal Court orders record $300 million penalties in ASIC's case over 'egregious' Union Standard and CFD operator misconduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: Traded as EuropeFX; a former authorised representative of Union Standard International Group found by the Federal Court to have engaged in systemic unconscionable conduct, part of the $300.2 million combined penalty action, with customers of EuropeFX and TradeFred together losing over $83 million.
risk_indicators: Found by the Federal Court to have engaged in systemic unconscionable conduct as an authorised representative of Union Standard, part of a $300.2 million combined penalty action

source_evidence.title: 26-117MR Federal Court orders record $300 million penalties in ASIC's case over 'egregious' Union Standard and CFD operator misconduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: Traded as TradeFred; a former authorised representative of Union Standard International Group found by the Federal Court to have engaged in systemic unconscionable conduct, part of the $300.2 million combined penalty action.

candidates.name: Qteq Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Oil and gas services company Qteq and its chairman collectively fined $6 million for attempted cartel conduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: Queensland-based oil and gas services company fined $5 million by the Federal Court (in proceedings brought by the ACCC) for attempted cartel conduct between 2017-2019, attempting to induce suppliers into arrangements including provisions not to supply services to large oil and gas companies and to rig a multi-million-dollar tender.
risk_indicators: Fined $5 million by the Federal Court for attempted cartel conduct (ACCC proceedings)
Its executive chairman was separately fined $1 million, described by the ACCC as the highest individual competition-law penalty in Australia

candidates.name: OneSteel Manufacturing Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Compliance update - January to March 2026 (Clean Energy Regulator, Safeguard Mechanism excess emissions situations)

candidates.public_interest_reason: Operator of the Whyalla Steelworks facility (currently in external administration, part of the former GFG Alliance group), named by the Clean Energy Regulator as one of three facilities in an excess emissions situation under the Safeguard Mechanism as at the 1 April 2026 compliance deadline. Completed a partial surrender of 198,003 units to reduce its excess position but remained non-compliant at the deadline.
risk_indicators: In an excess emissions situation under the Safeguard Mechanism as at the 1 April 2026 compliance deadline, per the Clean Energy Regulator's January-March 2026 compliance update
Completed only a partial unit surrender (198,003 units) toward its excess emissions position

candidates.name: Sunshine Loans Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 24-074MR Court finds Sunshine Loans charged customers prohibited fees
SunshineLoans Pty Ltd v Australian Securities and Investments Commission [2026] HCA 8
25-046MR Full Federal Court finds in favour of ASIC in two appeals concerning Sunshine Loans
26-052MR High Court finds in favour of ASIC on an appeal by Sunshine Loans

candidates.public_interest_reason: Federal Court found Sunshine Loans Pty Ltd contravened the National Credit Code and National Consumer Credit Protection Act 2009 by charging a prohibited $35 'amendment fee' on over 670,000 small amount credit contracts between 1 July 2016 and 2 November 2020, in proceedings brought by ASIC seeking $10.5 million in penalties. The company's application to have the trial judge recuse himself from the penalty hearing (on apprehended-bias grounds) was dismissed by the High Court of Australia on 18 March 2026 ([2026] HCA 8). Following the trial judge's recusal from the penalty phase (on apprehended-bias grounds relating to his credibility findings against Sunshine Loans and its witnesses), ASIC successfully appealed that recusal decision to the Full Federal Court (March 2025) and then, after Sunshine Loans' further appeal, to the High Court, which found unanimously (7-0) in ASIC's favour on 18 March 2026. The penalty hearing has now been remitted to the original trial judge, Justice Roger Derrington, for determination.
risk_indicators: Found by the Federal Court to have contravened the National Credit Code/NCCP Act by charging a prohibited fee on over 670,000 small amount credit contracts
Subject of ASIC penalty proceedings seeking $10.5 million
Unsuccessfully sought recusal of the trial judge from the penalty hearing; application dismissed by the High Court of Australia, [2026] HCA 8
Unanimous 7-0 High Court decision confirming the trial judge should not have recused himself from the penalty phase, following an earlier Full Federal Court win for ASIC on the same recusal question

candidates.name: Mercer Superannuation (Australia) Limited

source_evidence.title: 24-173MR ASIC's first greenwashing case results in landmark $11.3 million penalty for Mercer

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Mercer Superannuation (Australia) Limited to pay an $11.3 million penalty — ASIC's first successful greenwashing civil penalty case — after Mercer admitted to making misleading statements that seven 'Sustainable Plus' superannuation investment options excluded investments in carbon-intensive industries such as thermal coal, when adequate systems were not in place to ensure the ESG claims were accurate or that exclusions were enforced.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $11.3 million for greenwashing conduct, admitted by the company
Court also made an adverse publicity order requiring disclosure of the contraventions
Root cause found to be inadequate systems to verify and enforce ESG exclusion claims

candidates.name: Vanguard Investments Australia Ltd

source_evidence.title: 24-213MR ASIC's Vanguard greenwashing action results in record $12.9 million penalty

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Vanguard Investments Australia to pay a $12.9 million penalty — the largest greenwashing penalty imposed in Australia to date — after Vanguard admitted that approximately 74% of the securities (by market value) in its Ethically Conscious Global Aggregate Bond Index Fund had not actually been researched or screened against the ESG exclusion criteria it had told investors would be applied.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $12.9 million, the largest greenwashing penalty in Australia to date
Admitted approximately 74% of the fund's securities by market value were not researched or screened against its stated ESG criteria
This was ASIC's second successful greenwashing civil penalty action

candidates.name: LGSS Pty Ltd (trustee for Active Super)

source_evidence.title: 25-042MR Active Super ordered to pay $10.5 million penalty in ASIC's third greenwashing court action

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered LGSS Pty Ltd, as trustee for the Active Super fund, to pay a $10.5 million penalty — ASIC's third successful greenwashing civil penalty action — after finding Active Super had claimed in its marketing to eliminate investments posing environmental or community risk (including gambling, coal mining and oil tar sands) while continuing to hold direct investments in companies such as Shell Plc and Whitehaven Coal.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $10.5 million for false or misleading ESG representations
Found to have retained direct investments (e.g. Shell Plc, Whitehaven Coal) in categories it had publicly claimed to exclude
ASIC's third successful greenwashing civil penalty action

candidates.name: Fiducian Investment Management Services Limited

source_evidence.title: 25-225MR ASIC acts against ESG investment fund responsible entity alleging governance failures and misleading conduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: ASIC commenced its fourth greenwashing civil penalty action against Fiducian Investment Management Services Limited (FIMSL) as responsible entity of the Diversified Social Aspirations Fund, alleging FIMSL failed to act with care and diligence, made misleading statements in the fund's PDS about monitoring underlying investments, and failed to address investor concerns that the fund held investments in companies including BHP, Rio Tinto, Woodside Energy, Newcrest Mining and Orica. FIMSL has agreed to make admissions but a Statement of Agreed Facts and penalty had not been finalised or approved by the court as of the most recent reporting.
risk_indicators: Subject of ASIC's fourth greenwashing civil penalty action, alleging governance failures and misleading PDS statements
FIMSL has agreed to make admissions of contraventions of the Corporations Act and ASIC Act
Proceedings not yet concluded: no court-approved penalty as of the most recent reporting

candidates.name: Netwealth Superannuation Services Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: APRA accepts court enforceable undertaking from Netwealth

candidates.public_interest_reason: APRA accepted a court enforceable undertaking from Netwealth Superannuation Services Pty Ltd (trustee of a superannuation platform with around 115,000 member accounts and over $40 billion in funds under management) after a thematic review found material weaknesses in its investment governance framework, including deficiencies in onboarding due diligence and monitoring of investment options and management of outsourced investment services and related conflicts of interest. In parallel, ASIC commenced separate Corporations Act proceedings against Netwealth and accepted its own court-enforceable undertaking relating to First Guardian investor compensation.
risk_indicators: Subject of an APRA court enforceable undertaking following a thematic review identifying material weaknesses in investment governance
Findings included deficiencies in due diligence, monitoring of investment options, and management of related-party conflicts of interest
Also subject of separate, parallel ASIC Corporations Act proceedings and a court-enforceable undertaking concerning First Guardian investor compensation

candidates.name: United Super Pty Ltd (trustee for Cbus)

source_evidence.title: APRA accepts court enforceable undertaking from Cbus and launches investigation

candidates.public_interest_reason: APRA accepted a court enforceable undertaking from United Super Pty Ltd, trustee for the Construction and Building Unions Superannuation Fund (Cbus, over 920,000 member accounts and around $100 billion in funds under management), after a prudential review found significant and persistent weaknesses in its operational risk management framework across four areas: operational risk management, information/IT resilience, insurance claims processing and administration, and expenditure management. APRA separately launched an investigation into United Super for possible breaches of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993, focused on expenditure management practices.
risk_indicators: Subject of an APRA court enforceable undertaking following findings of significant and persistent operational risk management weaknesses
Undertaking spans four areas: operational risk management, IT resilience, insurance claims administration, and expenditure management
Separately under APRA investigation for possible breaches of the SIS Act relating to expenditure management practices

candidates.name: Fiducian Portfolio Services Limited

source_evidence.title: APRA imposes additional licence conditions on Fiducian

candidates.public_interest_reason: APRA imposed additional licence conditions (effective 2 April 2026) on Fiducian Portfolio Services Limited, trustee of the Fiducian Superannuation Fund (approximately 9,779 member accounts, over $3.1 billion in funds under management), after a review identified concerns including insufficiently rigorous investment selection criteria and due diligence, weak monitoring/reporting frameworks, management of related-party conflicts of interest, and deficiencies in board governance and oversight. Note: this is a distinct legal entity from Fiducian Investment Management Services Limited (T-AU-2026-00034), which is the subject of a separate ASIC greenwashing action against a different Fiducian-group entity.
risk_indicators: Subject of additional APRA licence conditions effective 2 April 2026 following a review of investment governance
Findings included insufficiently rigorous investment selection/due diligence criteria and weak monitoring frameworks
Findings also included deficiencies in board governance and management of related-party conflicts of interest

candidates.name: FIIG Securities Limited

source_evidence.title: 26-021MR ASIC action sees FIIG Securities ordered to pay $2.5 million over cyber security failures

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered FIIG Securities Limited to pay a $2.5 million penalty plus $500,000 towards ASIC's costs — the first civil penalty imposed on an Australian Financial Services licensee for cybersecurity-related contraventions of its general licensee obligations under section 912A of the Corporations Act 2001. FIIG admitted that over a four-year period it failed to have adequate cybersecurity measures in place, which contributed to a May 2023 cyberattack in which approximately 385GB of data, including sensitive client information, was exfiltrated from its systems.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $2.5 million, admitted by the company — the first such penalty for cybersecurity-related licensee obligation failures in Australia
Admitted inadequate cybersecurity measures over a four-year period prior to a May 2023 data breach affecting approximately 385GB of data including sensitive client information
Court also ordered an independent-expert compliance programme to review its cyber security and resilience systems

candidates.name: Reebelo Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Reebelo Australia pays penalties for alleged false or misleading statements about consumer guarantee rights

candidates.public_interest_reason: Reebelo Australia, an online marketplace for new and refurbished electronics, paid $59,400 in penalties after the ACCC issued three infringement notices alleging false or misleading statements about consumer guarantee rights. The statements purported to impose a 14-day time limit on consumers' ability to obtain a remedy for faulty, damaged, misdescribed or wrong-model goods, when the Australian Consumer Law entitles consumers to a remedy regardless of elapsed time. The ACCC noted particular concern given the products involved are often refurbished high-end electronics such as laptops and mobile phones.
risk_indicators: Paid $59,400 in penalties across three ACCC infringement notices for alleged false or misleading consumer-guarantee representations
Alleged representations imposed a 14-day limit on remedy rights that the Australian Consumer Law does not itself impose
ACCC identified potential financial harm given the higher-value, refurbished electronics products involved

candidates.name: Hancock Agriculture Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 25-298MR ASIC issues over $2.2 million in infringement notices to 12 large proprietary companies for alleged failure to lodge financial reports

candidates.public_interest_reason: Hancock Agriculture Pty Ltd was one of 12 large proprietary companies issued infringement notices by ASIC (published 11 December 2025) for allegedly failing to lodge audited FY24 financial reports on time, following a three-month ASIC surveillance campaign into non-lodgement by large proprietary companies. Penalties across the 12 companies ranged from $150,240 to $187,800 each, totalling over $2.2 million, all paid in full.
risk_indicators: Issued an ASIC infringement notice for alleged failure to lodge an audited FY24 financial report on time
One of 12 companies targeted in a dedicated ASIC surveillance campaign on large-proprietary-company non-lodgement
Penalty paid in full; payment of an infringement notice is not an admission of guilt or liability

candidates.name: S. Kidman & Co Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 25-298MR ASIC issues over $2.2 million in infringement notices to 12 large proprietary companies for alleged failure to lodge financial reports

candidates.public_interest_reason: S. Kidman & Co Pty Ltd, one of Australia's largest cattle station operators, was one of 12 large proprietary companies issued infringement notices by ASIC (published 11 December 2025) for allegedly failing to lodge audited FY24 financial reports on time, following a three-month ASIC surveillance campaign into non-lodgement by large proprietary companies. Penalties across the 12 companies ranged from $150,240 to $187,800 each, totalling over $2.2 million, all paid in full.
risk_indicators: Issued an ASIC infringement notice for alleged failure to lodge an audited FY24 financial report on time
One of 12 companies targeted in a dedicated ASIC surveillance campaign on large-proprietary-company non-lodgement
Penalty paid in full; payment of an infringement notice is not an admission of guilt or liability

candidates.name: MJ Bale Group

source_evidence.title: 25-298MR ASIC issues over $2.2 million in infringement notices to 12 large proprietary companies for alleged failure to lodge financial reports

candidates.public_interest_reason: MJ Bale Group was one of 12 large proprietary companies issued infringement notices by ASIC (published 11 December 2025) for allegedly failing to lodge audited financial reports on time, paying $198,000 (a higher amount reflecting an increase in the penalty unit value applicable at the time of the alleged contravention). MJ Bale has since lodged its outstanding FY22 through FY25 reports.
risk_indicators: Issued an ASIC infringement notice, paying $198,000, for alleged failure to lodge audited financial reports on time
One of 12 companies targeted in a dedicated ASIC surveillance campaign on large-proprietary-company non-lodgement
Has since lodged its outstanding FY22-FY25 reports; payment of an infringement notice is not an admission of guilt or liability

candidates.name: EnergyAustralia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: AER Infringement Notices - EnergyAustralia Pty Ltd - INF-20260001 and INF-20260002
EnergyAustralia to pay $14m for making misleading statements and breaching the Electricity Retail Code

candidates.public_interest_reason: EnergyAustralia Pty Ltd paid two AER infringement notices totalling $142,800 for alleged breaches of the National Energy Retail Law and National Energy Retail Rules. The AER alleged EnergyAustralia transferred an elderly customer's electricity account to itself without obtaining consent (after another person had mistakenly set up an account at that address), then sent bills and disconnection warnings, and disconnected the customer without following the required notice procedures under the Retail Rules. Separately, the Federal Court ordered EnergyAustralia to pay $14 million in penalties (the ACCC's first proceeding under the Electricity Retail Code since its 2019 introduction) after EnergyAustralia admitted sending misleading price-change communications to around 566,000 consumers between June and September 2022, including misrepresenting the estimated annual price of its electricity offer for an average customer and failing to state the lowest possible price, plus 27 non-compliant online electricity offers over the same period.
risk_indicators: Paid $142,800 across two AER infringement notices for alleged Retail Law and Retail Rules breaches
Alleged failure to obtain customer consent before transferring an electricity account
Alleged failure to follow required notice procedures before disconnecting an elderly customer
Federal Court penalty of $14 million, admitted, for misleading pricing communications sent to approximately 566,000 consumers — the ACCC's first Electricity Retail Code proceeding

candidates.name: Origin Energy Electricity Ltd

source_evidence.title: AER v Origin Energy Electricity Ltd & Ors
Origin penalised $17 million for customer hardship breaches

candidates.public_interest_reason: The AER instituted Federal Court proceedings on 19 December 2025 against Origin Energy Electricity Ltd and three other Origin subsidiaries, alleging the group continued to receive Centrepay payments from customers who had closed their accounts and fully paid what they owed, without informing or refunding them within the required timeframe — around 77,000 alleged breaches affecting over 3,400 customers between December 2019 and March 2025, totalling more than $2.5 million in retained overcharges. The AER also alleges Origin knew of the processing issue as early as 2017 and paused a process that could have prevented further overcharging, and separately failed to have adequate compliance-monitoring policies and systems in place. The AER is seeking penalties, declarations, customer remediation and a compliance program. Separately, the Federal Court found Origin Energy Electricity Ltd and other Origin entities breached hardship-customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Law/Rules on more than 100,000 occasions between January 2018 and October 2021, affecting more than 90,000 customers across NSW, the ACT, Queensland and South Australia, resulting in a $17 million penalty — the largest total penalty ever imposed for breaches of that law at the time — plus $200,000 in costs and a compliance program.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court proceedings instituted by the AER on 19 December 2025, alleging around 77,000 breaches of the National Energy Retail Rules/Law
Alleged to have retained more than $2.5 million in Centrepay overcharges from over 3,400 affected customers
Alleged to have known of the systems issue as early as 2017 and to have paused a process that could have prevented further overcharging
AER separately alleges a failure to have adequate policies, systems and procedures to monitor Retail Rules compliance
Separately found to have breached customer hardship obligations on more than 100,000 occasions, affecting over 90,000 customers, resulting in a $17 million penalty

candidates.name: Australia and New Zealand Banking Group Limited (ANZ)

source_evidence.title: 25-314MR Federal Court orders $250 million combined penalties against ANZ

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered ANZ to pay $250 million in combined penalties (delivered 19 December 2025, up from the $240 million ASIC and ANZ had jointly proposed) — the largest combined penalty ASIC has secured against a single entity. ANZ admitted to unconscionable conduct in services provided to the Australian government, incorrectly reporting bond trading data to the government by tens of billions of dollars, and widespread misconduct across products and services affecting nearly 65,000 customers.
risk_indicators: Ordered to pay $250 million in combined Federal Court penalties, the largest ASIC has secured against a single entity
Admitted unconscionable conduct in services provided to the Australian government, including incorrectly reporting bond trading data by tens of billions of dollars
Admitted widespread misconduct across products and services affecting nearly 65,000 customers

candidates.name: Macquarie Investment Management Limited

source_evidence.title: 26-053MR Federal Court declares Macquarie contravened the Corporations Act in relation to Shield Master Fund

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court declared that Macquarie Investment Management Limited (MIML), as superannuation platform trustee, contravened the Corporations Act by failing to place the Shield Master Fund on a watch list for heightened monitoring despite warning signs, after more than $480 million was invested in Shield by at least 5,800 consumers (mostly via superannuation platforms) since February 2022. Macquarie separately admitted contraventions and, under a court-enforceable undertaking, paid approximately $321 million to over 3,000 affected members — 100% of the amounts they invested less any withdrawals.
risk_indicators: Federal Court declared contraventions of the Corporations Act for failing to place a fund on a watch list for heightened monitoring despite warning signs
Paid approximately $321 million to over 3,000 affected members under a court-enforceable undertaking, following admitted contraventions
Fund at issue attracted more than $480 million from at least 5,800 consumers since February 2022 before the failures were addressed

candidates.name: BlueScope Steel Ltd

source_evidence.title: Court upholds record penalty in ACCC's cartel case against BlueScope

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Full Federal Court dismissed BlueScope Steel Ltd's appeal against a $57.5 million civil penalty — the highest penalty ever imposed for cartel conduct or a competition-law breach in Australia — for attempting to induce eight steel distributors and an overseas manufacturer to fix and raise pricing for flat steel products supplied in Australia between September 2013 and June 2014, in ACCC proceedings first instituted in August 2019. A former BlueScope general manager was separately penalised $500,000 in the same proceedings; that individual is noted here as context only and is not recorded as a separate candidate.
risk_indicators: Highest penalty ever imposed for cartel conduct or a competition-law breach in Australia ($57.5 million), upheld on appeal by the Full Federal Court
Found to have attempted to induce eight steel distributors and an overseas manufacturer to fix and raise flat-steel pricing
Federal Court proceedings ran from institution in August 2019 through a 2022 cartel-conduct finding to the 2023 penalty and 2025 appeal dismissal

candidates.name: Perfection Fresh Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: ACCC takes court action against fresh produce suppliers for alleged price fixing cartel

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced Federal Court civil proceedings against Perfection Fresh Australia (Australia's second-largest fresh vegetable supplier) alongside three other suppliers and three senior executives, alleging price fixing on 84 occasions in the supply of fresh produce (including broccoli, cauliflower, iceberg lettuce, cucumber, Brussels sprouts and zucchini) to ALDI stores in NSW, Victoria and Queensland between 2018 and 2024.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court civil cartel proceedings instituted by the ACCC, alleging price fixing on 84 occasions
Alleged conduct concerns supply of fresh produce to ALDI across three states between 2018 and 2024
Proceedings are ongoing; allegations not yet determined by the court

candidates.name: Hydro Produce (Aust) Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: ACCC takes court action against fresh produce suppliers for alleged price fixing cartel

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced Federal Court civil proceedings against Hydro Produce (Aust) alongside three other suppliers and three senior executives, alleging price fixing on 84 occasions in the supply of fresh produce (including broccoli, cauliflower, iceberg lettuce, cucumber, Brussels sprouts and zucchini) to ALDI stores in NSW, Victoria and Queensland between 2018 and 2024.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court civil cartel proceedings instituted by the ACCC, alleging price fixing on 84 occasions
Alleged conduct concerns supply of fresh produce to ALDI across three states between 2018 and 2024
Proceedings are ongoing; allegations not yet determined by the court

candidates.name: Veli Velisha Fresh Produce / Velisha National Farms

source_evidence.title: ACCC takes court action against fresh produce suppliers for alleged price fixing cartel

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced Federal Court civil proceedings against Veli Velisha Fresh Produce and Velisha National Farms, alongside two other suppliers and named senior executives (its director/CEO and a senior sales manager, noted here as context only and not recorded as separate candidates), alleging price fixing on 84 occasions in the supply of fresh produce to ALDI stores in NSW, Victoria and Queensland between 2018 and 2024.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court civil cartel proceedings instituted by the ACCC, alleging price fixing on 84 occasions
Alleged conduct concerns supply of fresh produce to ALDI across three states between 2018 and 2024
Proceedings are ongoing; allegations not yet determined by the court

candidates.name: M. Fragapane & Sons Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: ACCC takes court action against fresh produce suppliers for alleged price fixing cartel

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced Federal Court civil proceedings against M. Fragapane & Sons, alongside three other suppliers and named senior executives (its general sales manager, noted here as context only and not recorded as a separate candidate), alleging price fixing on 84 occasions in the supply of fresh produce to ALDI stores in NSW, Victoria and Queensland between 2018 and 2024.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court civil cartel proceedings instituted by the ACCC, alleging price fixing on 84 occasions
Alleged conduct concerns supply of fresh produce to ALDI across three states between 2018 and 2024
Proceedings are ongoing; allegations not yet determined by the court

candidates.name: AMP Superannuation Limited and N.M. Superannuation Proprietary Limited (AMP Super)

source_evidence.title: APRA agrees to court enforceable undertaking from AMP Super over compliance deficiencies

candidates.public_interest_reason: APRA accepted a court enforceable undertaking from AMP Superannuation Limited and N.M. Superannuation Proprietary Limited (collectively AMP Super, which controls two superannuation funds with estimated net assets of $116 billion) to address governance and risk-management deficiencies, following a lengthy APRA investigation into past conduct that APRA believes led to a number of potential breaches of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993.
risk_indicators: Subject of an APRA court enforceable undertaking addressing governance and risk-management deficiencies
Undertaking followed a lengthy APRA investigation into past conduct believed to involve potential SIS Act breaches
Trustee entities control superannuation funds with estimated net assets of $116 billion

candidates.name: Money3 Loans Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 26-084MR Federal Court orders Money3 to pay $1.55 million penalty for responsible lending breaches

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Money3 Loans Pty Ltd to pay $1.55 million in penalties for breaching responsible lending obligations in providing car finance to vulnerable consumers largely or solely reliant on Centrelink payments. For five loans entered into between May 2019 and February 2021, the Court found Money3 did not make reasonable inquiries about or verify borrowers' living expenses using bank statement data it held, and in one instance failed to make reasonable inquiries about a borrower's requirements and objectives. ASIC commenced proceedings on 17 May 2023.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $1.55 million for breaching responsible lending obligations
Found to have failed to make reasonable inquiries about or verify borrowers' living expenses for five car-finance loans
Affected borrowers were largely or solely reliant on Centrelink payments

candidates.name: Australian Unity Funds Management Limited

source_evidence.title: 26-011MR Federal Court orders Australian Unity Funds Management to pay $7 million penalty for failing to confirm product suitability for investors

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Australian Unity Funds Management Limited to pay a $7.125 million penalty for breaching design and distribution obligations as responsible entity of the Australian Unity Select Income Fund. AUFM admitted it failed to take reasonable steps to ensure interests in the Fund were only distributed to investors matching its Target Market Determinations: of 328 non-advised investors, 89 were not required to submit a suitability questionnaire, and of the 239 who did, up to 144 gave at least one response suggesting they were outside the target market. The Court also ordered a written adverse publicity notice to impacted investors and payment of ASIC's costs.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $7.125 million, admitted, for breaching design and distribution obligations
Up to 144 of 239 responding investors gave at least one response suggesting they were outside the fund's target market
Court additionally ordered a written adverse publicity notice to impacted investors

candidates.name: First Mutual Private Equity Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 26-153MR Federal Court orders First Mutual Private Equity and unregistered managed investment scheme to be wound up

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered on 13 July 2026 that First Mutual Private Equity Pty Ltd and an unregistered managed investment scheme operated by it and an individual be wound up, following an ASIC application, and appointed independent liquidators. ASIC's investigation indicates that between January 2020 and August 2025, around $131 million was raised from investors purportedly to be invested on the ASX. The winding-up was sought to enable orderly administration of remaining assets and, where possible, return of funds to investors.
risk_indicators: Subject of a Federal Court winding-up order obtained on ASIC's application, with independent liquidators appointed
ASIC's investigation indicates approximately $131 million was raised from investors between January 2020 and August 2025
Winding-up sought specifically to identify and return available funds to investors

candidates.name: Clorox Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Clorox ordered to pay $8.25m in penalties for misleading 'ocean plastic' claims about certain GLAD products

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Clorox Australia Pty Ltd to pay $8.25 million in penalties — the ACCC's first court greenwashing action since naming it a compliance priority in 2022/23 — for representing on packaging that its GLAD to be GREEN '50% Ocean Plastic Recycled' kitchen and garbage bags were made of at least 50% recycled plastic collected from the ocean or sea, when the plastic was instead collected from communities in Indonesia without formal waste management systems, up to 50km from a shoreline. More than 2.2 million units were supplied under this packaging between June 2021 and July 2023. Clorox cooperated with the ACCC, made admissions, and agreed the penalty jointly with the regulator.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $8.25 million, admitted, for misleading environmental packaging claims
More than 2.2 million units supplied under the misleading packaging over roughly two years
Court also ordered an ACL compliance program and a corrective notice on Clorox's website and social media

candidates.name: Edgewell Personal Care Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Banana Boat and Hawaiian Tropic owner in Court over alleged greenwashing claims that its sunscreens were 'reef friendly'

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced Federal Court proceedings against Edgewell Personal Care Australia Pty Ltd and its US parent, Edgewell Personal Care Company, alleging false or misleading 'reef friendly' claims made on Banana Boat and Hawaiian Tropic sunscreen products between August 2020 and December 2024 (the claim was removed from Australian marketing in October 2023). The ACCC alleges the no-harm representations were misleading because the products contained ingredients that cause or risk causing harm to reefs, including coral and marine life. The ACCC is seeking declarations, penalties, injunctions, a corrective notice, a compliance program and costs.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court proceedings commenced by the ACCC alleging misleading environmental ('reef friendly') claims
Alleged conduct spanned over four years of marketing before the claim was removed
Proceedings are ongoing; allegations not yet determined by the court

candidates.name: HSBC Bank Australia Limited

source_evidence.title: 26-127MR Federal Court orders $35 million penalty against HSBC for scam protection failures

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered HSBC Bank Australia Limited to pay a $35 million penalty — one of the first cases of its kind globally — after the bank admitted serious failures protecting customers from scams. Between January 2020 and August 2024, HSBC received more than 1,000 reports of unauthorised transactions totalling $34.6 million, and admitted inadequate controls on its internal transfer system between May 2023 and May 2024, a 380% surge in unauthorised-transaction reports in FY24, and average investigation times of 144 days. Approximately $21.5 million in compensation had been paid to affected customers by the time of judgment, with further payments expected, plus an additional $6.5 million in recovered funds returned.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $35 million, admitted, for scam-protection failures — one of the first cases of its kind globally
Admitted inadequate internal transfer system controls and average scam-investigation times of 144 days
More than 1,000 unauthorised-transaction reports totalling $34.6 million between January 2020 and August 2024

candidates.name: ASX Limited

source_evidence.title: 26-143MR ASX ordered to pay $20.5 million penalty for misleading conduct relating to CHESS replacement project

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered ASX Limited, operator of Australia's national securities exchange, to pay a $20.5 million penalty after admitting a 10 February 2022 market announcement stating its CHESS replacement project was 'progressing well' was misleading, in contravention of the ASIC Act. Six weeks after the announcement ASX acknowledged a strong likelihood of delays, and by November 2022 the project (intended to replace critical clearing/settlement infrastructure using distributed ledger technology) was paused entirely, with ASX writing off approximately $245-255 million in project costs. ASX was also ordered to pay $3 million toward ASIC's costs.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $20.5 million, admitted, for misleading conduct concerning critical market infrastructure
Misleading statement concerned a project underpinning Australia's securities clearing and settlement system
Project was subsequently paused with approximately $245-255 million in costs written off

candidates.name: Australian Clinical Labs Limited

source_evidence.title: Australian Clinical Labs ordered to pay penalties in relation to Medlab Pathology data breach in first for Privacy Act

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Australian Clinical Labs Limited (ACL) to pay $5.8 million in civil penalties — the first civil penalties ever ordered under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) — in relation to a February 2022 data breach of its Medlab Pathology business, in which a threat actor exfiltrated approximately 86 gigabytes of data including the personal information of at least 223,000 individuals. The penalty comprised $4.2 million for failing to take reasonable steps to protect personal information (APP 11.1), $800,000 for failing to carry out a reasonable and expeditious assessment of whether an eligible data breach had occurred, and $800,000 for failing to properly notify the Australian Information Commissioner of the breach.
risk_indicators: First-ever civil penalty ordered under the Privacy Act 1988, totalling $5.8 million
Data breach exfiltrated personal information of at least 223,000 individuals
Penalty included separate contraventions for inadequate breach assessment and failure to properly notify the regulator

candidates.name: The Star Pty Limited

source_evidence.title: AUSTRAC commences proceedings in the Federal Court against Star Entertainment Group entities

candidates.public_interest_reason: AUSTRAC commenced civil penalty proceedings in the Federal Court against The Star Pty Limited alleging serious and systemic non-compliance with Australia's anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing (AML/CTF) laws, including poor governance, failures of risk management, and failure to carry out appropriate ongoing customer due diligence over a number of years. The investigation followed a proactive AUSTRAC industry-wide compliance campaign beginning September 2019, with an enforcement investigation opened in June 2021. As of March 2026, the Federal Court had not yet delivered judgment, with AUSTRAC noting its own submissions were completed in mid-2025.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court civil penalty proceedings brought by AUSTRAC alleging serious and systemic AML/CTF non-compliance
Alleged failures include poor governance, inadequate risk management, and failure to conduct ongoing customer due diligence over a number of years
Proceedings remain undetermined as of March 2026, more than four years after the enforcement investigation was opened

candidates.name: The Star Entertainment QLD Limited

source_evidence.title: AUSTRAC commences proceedings in the Federal Court against Star Entertainment Group entities

candidates.public_interest_reason: AUSTRAC commenced civil penalty proceedings in the Federal Court against The Star Entertainment QLD Limited alleging serious and systemic non-compliance with Australia's anti-money laundering and counter-terrorism financing (AML/CTF) laws, including poor governance, failures of risk management, and failure to carry out appropriate ongoing customer due diligence over a number of years. The investigation followed a proactive AUSTRAC industry-wide compliance campaign beginning September 2019, with an enforcement investigation opened in June 2021. As of March 2026, the Federal Court had not yet delivered judgment.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court civil penalty proceedings brought by AUSTRAC alleging serious and systemic AML/CTF non-compliance
Alleged failures include poor governance, inadequate risk management, and failure to conduct ongoing customer due diligence over a number of years
Proceedings remain undetermined as of March 2026, more than four years after the enforcement investigation was opened

candidates.name: BPS Financial Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 26-008MR BPS Financial to pay $14 million in penalties over crypto Qoin Wallet

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered BPS Financial Pty Ltd to pay $14 million in penalties ($2 million for unlicensed conduct, $12 million for misleading and deceptive conduct) after finding it issued and advised on its Qoin Wallet crypto product for almost three years without holding an Australian Financial Services Licence, and made false and misleading representations about the product, including as to token convertibility and regulatory approval. During the misconduct period (2020-2023) BPS issued more than 96,000 Qoin Wallets and generated over $42 million in revenue from Qoin token sales. The company was also permanently restrained from further misleading claims and barred from operating a financial services business without a licence for ten years.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $14 million for unlicensed conduct and misleading/deceptive representations
Found to have operated without an Australian Financial Services Licence for almost three years, issuing more than 96,000 crypto wallets
Permanently restrained from further misleading claims and barred from operating a financial services business without a licence for ten years

candidates.name: Optus Mobile Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Federal Court orders Optus to pay $100m penalty for unconscionable conduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court approved a $100 million penalty against Optus Mobile Pty Ltd for unconscionable conduct, after sales staff at 16 retail stores pressured over 400 vulnerable consumers between August 2019 and July 2023 into purchasing telecommunications products and services they did not want, need or could afford. Affected consumers included people with mental disability, diminished cognitive capacity, limited financial literacy, English not as a first language, and many First Nations Australians from regional, remote and very remote areas. The Court found commission-based sales arrangements had clear potential to incentivise the exploitative conduct.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $100 million for unconscionable conduct, one of the largest ACCC-related consumer penalties
Affected more than 400 consumers, many experiencing vulnerability or disadvantage, over a four-year period
Court found commission-based sales incentive structures had clear potential to exacerbate consumer vulnerability

candidates.name: ARMA Group Holdings Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: ARMA Group and Force Legal in Court over more than 320,000 allegedly misleading debt notices

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced Federal Court proceedings against debt-collection agency ARMA Group Holdings Pty Ltd (owned by ASX-listed Credit Clear Limited), alleging it issued more than 320,000 letters, emails and text messages between February 2022 and September 2025 falsely suggesting consumers were legally required to pay debts, including debts that were no longer outstanding or were statute-barred. Credit Clear, ARMA and Force Legal (a related legal practice) deny the allegations and intend to defend the proceedings.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court civil proceedings alleging over 320,000 misleading debt-collection communications
Alleged to have represented debts as due when they were statute-barred or no longer outstanding
Proceedings are ongoing and defended; allegations not yet determined by the court

candidates.name: Force Legal Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: ARMA Group and Force Legal in Court over more than 320,000 allegedly misleading debt notices

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced Federal Court proceedings against legal practice Force Legal Pty Ltd (owned by ASX-listed Credit Clear Limited, alongside related debt collector ARMA Group), alleging it sent letters containing misrepresentations about the steps it would take to escalate debt-recovery matters and the consequences of non-payment, including warnings that wages could be deducted without court proceedings and claims consumers would automatically be liable for legal costs and interest. Credit Clear, ARMA and Force Legal deny the allegations and intend to defend the proceedings.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court civil proceedings alleging misrepresentations in debt-recovery correspondence
Alleged misrepresentations included consequences of non-payment not guaranteed under Australian law
Proceedings are ongoing and defended; allegations not yet determined by the court

candidates.name: Coles Supermarkets Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Court finds that Coles misled customers over 'Down Down' claims
ACCC opposes Coles' acquisition of a supermarket and liquor site in Kalgoorlie

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court found Coles Supermarkets Australia made false or misleading representations about 'Down Down' discounts on 245 common products between February 2022 and May 2023, in proceedings instituted by the ACCC in September 2024. The Court found Coles temporarily raised prices by at least 15% for a short period before applying a 'Down Down' promotion at a price still higher than or equal to the pre-increase price, and found misleading representations in 13 of the 14 ticket types considered at the liability hearing. Penalties and other orders remain to be determined. Separately, on 1 July 2026 the ACCC determined — its first outright refusal under Australia's new mandatory merger control regime (effective from 1 January 2026) — that Coles' proposed acquisition of a lease for a vacant supermarket and liquor site in Kalgoorlie-Boulder would likely substantially lessen competition in the retail supply of groceries there, finding it would likely lead to the exit of an effective independent full-line competitor from the market.
risk_indicators: Federal Court liability finding of misleading conduct in 13 of 14 discount-ticket types examined
Conduct concerned pricing representations across 245 common supermarket products over 15 months
Penalty phase not yet determined as of the liability judgment
Subject of the ACCC's first outright merger refusal under the new mandatory regime, over a proposed Kalgoorlie site acquisition found likely to substantially lessen grocery competition

candidates.name: Woolworths Group Limited

source_evidence.title: ACCC takes Woolworths and Coles to court over alleged misleading 'Prices Dropped' and 'Down Down' claims

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC commenced separate Federal Court proceedings against Woolworths Group Limited alleging false or misleading 'Prices Dropped' representations on 266 products between September 2021 and May 2023, mirroring the parallel Coles matter: products allegedly had prices raised by at least 15% for a brief period before being placed in a 'Prices Dropped' promotion at a price still higher than or equal to the pre-rise price. As of the most recent reporting, proceedings were ongoing without a determined liability finding.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court civil proceedings alleging misleading discount-pricing representations across 266 products
Alleged conduct spanned September 2021 to May 2023
Proceedings are ongoing; allegations not yet determined by the court

candidates.name: Block Earner (Web3 Ventures Pty Ltd)

source_evidence.title: 26-124MR ASIC successful in High Court Block Earner appeal

candidates.public_interest_reason: The High Court unanimously (7-0) found that digital asset service provider Block Earner engaged in unlicensed financial services conduct by offering its fixed-yield digital asset product 'Earner' (offered March-November 2022) without an Australian Financial Services Licence, overturning the Full Federal Court's 2025 decision that Earner was not a financial product. ASIC commenced civil penalty proceedings on 23 November 2022; the Federal Court found unlicensed conduct in February 2024 but relieved Block Earner of any penalty in June 2024, a relief decision ASIC appealed. The High Court's June 2026 ruling on the licensing question sends the matter back to the Full Federal Court to determine ASIC's appeal against the penalty relief.
risk_indicators: High Court unanimously found unlicensed financial services conduct in offering a fixed-yield digital asset product
Federal Court's earlier decision to relieve the company of any penalty is now subject to further appeal following the High Court ruling
Matter remains before the courts pending the Full Federal Court's determination of the penalty question

candidates.name: Metropolitan Collieries Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Metropolitan Collieries to pay over $500,000 after polluting creek with coal

candidates.public_interest_reason: The NSW Land and Environment Court fined Metropolitan Collieries Pty Ltd (operator of the Helensburgh mine, part of the Peabody Energy group) $196,560 plus $304,806 in the NSW EPA's legal and investigation costs, after the company pleaded guilty to water pollution and licence breach offences. The EPA prosecuted two incidents in September and October 2022 in which the company's failure to maintain the Turkeys Nest Dam caused it to overflow, discharging water contaminated with coal fines and sediment into Camp Gully Creek, which flows into the Hacking River system adjoining the Royal National Park. $150,000 of the penalty is allocated to fund water-quality and environmental monitoring in the Royal National Park and Garawarra State Conservation Area.
risk_indicators: Pleaded guilty to water pollution and mining licence breach offences in the NSW Land and Environment Court
Total financial consequence exceeding $500,000 including penalty and prosecution costs
Pollution incidents affected a waterway flowing into the Royal National Park and Garawarra State Conservation Area

candidates.name: Bendigo and Adelaide Bank Limited

source_evidence.title: APRA and AUSTRAC take action in response to risk management deficiencies at Bendigo and Adelaide Bank

candidates.public_interest_reason: APRA applied a $50 million operational risk capital charge on Bendigo and Adelaide Bank Limited, effective 1 January 2026, reducing its Level 2 Common Equity Tier 1 ratio by approximately 17 basis points, while AUSTRAC commenced an enforcement investigation into potential serious contraventions of the AML/CTF Act. The action followed an independent Deloitte review into suspected money laundering at a Bendigo Bank branch, which found significant deficiencies in the bank's identification, mitigation and management of money laundering and terrorism financing risk, with regulators concerned the weaknesses may extend more broadly across the bank's operations.
risk_indicators: APRA capital charge of $50 million imposed for operational risk deficiencies
AUSTRAC enforcement investigation commenced over potential serious AML/CTF Act contraventions
Independent review found significant deficiencies in money-laundering and terrorism-financing risk management, with regulators concerned issues may be systemic across the bank

candidates.name: Fewstone Pty Ltd (trading as City Beach)

source_evidence.title: City Beach ordered to pay $14 million in penalties for supplying non-compliant button battery products

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Fewstone Pty Ltd, trading as City Beach, to pay $14 million in penalties in the ACCC's first proceeding alleging breaches of the mandatory button battery safety and information standards. Between June 2022 and October 2024, City Beach supplied products (including toys, digital notepads, keyrings, lights and light-up Jibbitz accessories, many marketed to children) that did not comply with the safety standard on more than 54,000 occasions and the information standard on more than 56,000 occasions. The Court found 'pervasive failures' by City Beach to inform itself of its Australian Consumer Law obligations, noting the conduct exposed more than 50,000 young children to the risk of severe injury or death. The Court also ordered a compliance programme and corrective advertising.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $14 million — the first judgment under the button battery safety standards
Court found 'pervasive failures' to comply with safety requirements across more than 110,000 combined non-compliant supplies
Court found the conduct exposed more than 50,000 young children to risk of severe injury or death

candidates.name: Yamaha Motor Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: ACCC opposes Yamaha's proposed acquisition of Telwater

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC opposed Yamaha Motor Australia Pty Ltd's proposed acquisition of Telwater Pty Ltd (announced 18 December 2025) following an in-depth investigation, finding it likely to substantially lessen competition in the wholesale supply of outboard motors in Australia. The ACCC's concern centred on the combined entity's ability and incentive to leverage Telwater's dominant position (60-70% market share) in aluminium trailer boats to disadvantage rival outboard motor suppliers through bundling or tying strategies, which the ACCC considered likely to leave consumers with lower quality, higher prices and fewer brand choices.
risk_indicators: Subject of an ACCC merger opposition decision following an in-depth competition investigation
ACCC found the proposed acquisition likely to substantially lessen competition in outboard motor wholesale supply
ACCC identified a specific risk of bundling/tying strategies leveraging a proposed acquisition target's dominant market position

candidates.name: Telwater Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: ACCC opposes Yamaha's proposed acquisition of Telwater

candidates.public_interest_reason: The ACCC opposed Yamaha Motor Australia's proposed acquisition of Telwater Pty Ltd (announced 18 December 2025) following an in-depth investigation, finding it likely to substantially lessen competition in the wholesale supply of outboard motors in Australia. Telwater, market leader in aluminium trailer boats (approximately 60-70% market share via its Quintrex, Stacer and Yellowfin brands), was the acquisition target; the ACCC's concern centred on the combined entity's ability to leverage that dominant position to disadvantage rival outboard motor suppliers.
risk_indicators: Named as the acquisition target in an ACCC merger opposition decision following an in-depth competition investigation
Holds an approximately 60-70% market share in aluminium trailer boats, a position the ACCC found could be leveraged anti-competitively if the acquisition proceeded
ACCC found the proposed transaction likely to substantially lessen competition in outboard motor wholesale supply

candidates.name: Diamond Energy Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Diamond Energy pays penalties for failing to adequately communicate pricing information to consumers

candidates.public_interest_reason: Diamond Energy Pty Ltd paid $46,950 in penalties across three ACCC infringement notices for breaching the Electricity Retail Code, after admitting that in June 2024 it sent price-change communications to 12,809 customers that failed to include mandatory pricing information (including the lowest possible price). The ACCC also accepted a court-enforceable undertaking under which Diamond Energy committed to update its price-change notification templates and implement a compliance program.
risk_indicators: Paid $46,950 across three ACCC infringement notices for alleged Electricity Retail Code breaches
Admitted failing to include mandatory pricing information in communications sent to 12,809 customers
Gave the ACCC a court-enforceable undertaking to update notification templates and implement a compliance program

candidates.name: Medtronic Australasia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Medtronic Australasia Pty Ltd ordered to pay $22 million - the largest ever penalty for unlawful supply of therapeutic goods

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Medtronic Australasia Pty Ltd to pay $22 million in penalties — the largest ever imposed under the Therapeutic Goods Act 1989 — for unlawfully supplying 16,267 units of its Infuse Bone Graft Kit without the separately packaged LT Cage between September 2015 and January 2020, when the Australian Register of Therapeutic Goods entry only permitted supply of the two products together. The TGA commenced proceedings in August 2021; Medtronic was also ordered to pay $1 million toward the TGA's legal costs.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $22 million, the largest ever imposed under the Therapeutic Goods Act 1989
Found to have unlawfully supplied more than 16,000 units of a medical device kit outside its permitted ARTG registration over a four-and-a-half-year period
Supplied the non-compliant kits to a large number of hospitals

candidates.name: Philips Electronics Australia Limited

source_evidence.title: Court proceedings initiated against Philips Electronics Australia Limited

candidates.public_interest_reason: The TGA commenced Federal Court proceedings against Philips Electronics Australia Limited alleging the unlawful supply of sleep apnoea and breathing-support devices that did not meet Australian safety and performance requirements. The devices contained a foam (PE-PUR) at risk of degrading and releasing particulates that could be inhaled or ingested, with potential harm including respiratory tract irritation, inflammatory response and effects on the reproductive system. The TGA alleges more than 44,000 separate instances of unlawful supply between June 2019 and October 2022 (depending on device), and separately issued ten infringement notices totalling $133,200.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court proceedings alleging unlawful supply of medical devices not meeting safety and performance requirements
TGA alleges more than 44,000 separate instances of unlawful supply over a three-year period
Devices at issue contained foam with a real risk of degrading and releasing particulates that could be inhaled or ingested by patients
Also issued ten TGA infringement notices totalling $133,200

candidates.name: Telstra Group Limited

source_evidence.title: Telstra to pay $50m penalty for unconscionable sales to Indigenous consumers

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Telstra to pay $50 million — the second-highest penalty ever imposed under the Australian Consumer Law — for unconscionable conduct after sales staff at five licensed Telstra-branded stores in the Northern Territory, South Australia and Western Australia signed up 108 Indigenous consumers to multiple post-paid mobile contracts between January 2016 and August 2018 that they did not understand and could not afford. The Court found staff manipulated credit assessments, misrepresented products as free, and exploited customers' social, language, literacy and cultural vulnerabilities, leaving them with debts to Telstra ranging from $1,600 to almost $20,000. Telstra also gave the ACCC an enforceable undertaking to remediate affected consumers, improve its compliance program and expand its Indigenous telephone hotline and digital literacy program.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $50 million, the second-highest ever under the Australian Consumer Law
Found to have exploited the social, language, literacy and cultural vulnerabilities of 108 Indigenous consumers across multiple states/territories
Enforceable undertaking includes consumer remediation and expansion of Indigenous-specific support services

candidates.name: Jump Loops Pty Ltd (in liquidation, trading as Jump Swim)

source_evidence.title: Jump Swim ordered to pay penalties of $23 million and Ian Campbell ordered to pay compensation

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Jump Loops Pty Ltd, trading as Jump Swim (now in liquidation), to pay $23 million in penalties for falsely representing to 174 franchisees that they would have an operational swim school within 12 months of signing a franchise agreement — most never received one — and for accepting payments from 127 franchisees when it knew or ought to have known there was no reasonable basis to expect it could deliver within that timeframe. The company's founder and former managing director, Ian Michael Campbell, was separately ordered to pay $500,000 in compensation to franchisees and a $400,000 personal penalty, and was restrained from acting as a franchisor for three years.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $23 million against the corporate franchisor
Found to have accepted payments from 127 franchisees despite knowing it likely could not deliver an operational business within the represented timeframe
Company is now in liquidation; its founder was separately penalised and restrained from future franchisor activity

candidates.name: M2 Energy Pty Ltd (trading as Dodo Power & Gas)

source_evidence.title: Dodo pays $406,800 penalty for alleged breaches of hardship and payment plan obligations

candidates.public_interest_reason: M2 Energy Pty Ltd, trading as Dodo Power & Gas, paid $406,800 across six AER infringement notices for alleged breaches of the National Energy Retail Rules protecting customers experiencing hardship: failing to inform three hardship customers of its hardship policy, and failing to have regard to three customers' capacity to pay when establishing payment plans. The AER also accepted a court-enforceable undertaking in which Dodo admitted the breaches and acknowledged broader concerns about its hardship, payment-plan and direct-debit compliance systems.
risk_indicators: Paid $406,800 across six AER infringement notices for alleged hardship-obligation breaches
Admitted breaches under a court-enforceable undertaking, alongside acknowledged broader compliance-system concerns
Found by AER's compliance review to have failed to inform hardship customers of its hardship policy

candidates.name: Red Energy Pty Limited

source_evidence.title: Red Energy fined for allegedly removing hardship protections

candidates.public_interest_reason: Red Energy Pty Limited paid $474,600 in penalties and gave the AER a court-enforceable undertaking after allegedly failing to maintain its hardship policy and offer suitable payment plans for up to 371 customers who had been receiving hardship assistance but lost that status upon moving to a new residence while remaining a Red Energy customer, leaving them vulnerable to disconnection, debt collection and credit default listings.
risk_indicators: Paid $474,600 in AER penalties for alleged hardship-policy and payment-plan failures
Alleged to have failed to identify and assist up to 371 hardship customers who moved residence
Consumers left vulnerable to disconnection, debt collection and credit default listings as a result

candidates.name: Equity Trustees Superannuation Limited

source_evidence.title: 25-176MR ASIC sues Equity Trustees alleging due diligence failures relating to Shield
26-101MR ASIC sues Equity Trustees alleging First Guardian onboarding failures

candidates.public_interest_reason: ASIC commenced two separate Federal Court civil penalty proceedings against Equity Trustees Superannuation Limited as superannuation platform trustee: one alleging due diligence failures overseeing around $160 million of retirement savings invested into the Shield Master Fund during 2023-2024, and a second alleging it allowed about 2,700 members of the NQ Super & Pension fund to invest more than $65 million in First Guardian between June 2023 and March 2024 without obtaining critical documents (the fund's constitution, audited financial statements, compliance plan audit), and permitted members to invest 100% of their superannuation balances despite evidence the fund was or may have been illiquid. Both matters are part of ASIC's broader 2026 enforcement priority into the collapse of First Guardian and related funds, through which more than $420 million has been repaid to investors to date.
risk_indicators: Subject of two separate Federal Court civil penalty proceedings brought by ASIC, both ongoing
Alleged to have permitted members to invest without obtaining critical fund documents including audited financial statements
Alleged to have permitted 100% superannuation-balance investment in a fund despite evidence of possible illiquidity
Proceedings are part of a broader regulatory response in which more than $420 million has been repaid to affected investors to date

candidates.name: HTFS Nominees Pty Limited

source_evidence.title: APRA imposes additional licence conditions on HTFS Nominees Pty Limited

candidates.public_interest_reason: APRA imposed additional licence conditions on HTFS Nominees Pty Limited, trustee of the HUB24 Super Fund (approximately 165,000 member accounts and more than $55 billion in funds under management), following an APRA thematic review of investment governance, strategic planning and member-outcomes practices across superannuation platform trustees. The conditions require appointment of an independent expert to review the fund's platform investment menus and its frameworks for investment governance, conflicts management, strategic objectives and member outcomes. The conditions apply notwithstanding HUB24 Limited's pending acquisition of HTFS from EQT Holdings.
risk_indicators: Subject of additional APRA licence conditions following a regulator thematic review
Required to appoint an independent expert to review investment governance and conflicts-management frameworks
Trustee for a fund holding more than $55 billion in member retirement savings across roughly 165,000 accounts
Conditions imposed during a pending change-of-ownership transaction for the trustee entity
